WA3853 Lab Guide

CSS Fundamentals for Leaders

Evergreen Insurance Quote Page Brand Refresh with CSS Variables

Lab 1

In this lab, you will open the Evergreen Insurance Quote Page in Visual Studio Code and refactor its stylesheet to use CSS variables. You will then change a single variable value and watch the entire site rebrand from Evergreen green to a new corporate blue. Along the way you will use Chrome DevTools to inspect the box model and adjust spacing.

You are not writing new visual design this week. You are moving existing colors and spacing values into a central place (CSS variables) so they can be changed in one line. This is exactly the work a design-system engineer does when the brand team requests a palette change.

1.1. A Note About Keyboard Shortcuts

Your lab VM runs inside your browser. Some keyboard shortcuts (like Ctrl+Shift+X, Ctrl+S, Ctrl+F) may not always reach the VM — sometimes your browser catches them first. If a shortcut doesn’t do anything, that’s not you doing something wrong; just use the mouse/menu alternative that appears next to every shortcut in this lab. Every step in this lab lists both.

1.2. Why You’re Doing This Lab

CSS variables are one of the highest-leverage concepts in front-end work. A stylesheet with hardcoded colors is maintainable — until the brand team announces a repaint, at which point someone has to hunt every copy of #0f5132 across dozens of files, update each one, and hope they got them all. A stylesheet with variables turns that multi-week project into a one-line change.

This lab shows you that exact transformation on a real (small) page. By the end you will have turned a stylesheet that repeats the brand green seven or eight times into a stylesheet that has the color in exactly one place — and then you’ll change that one place from green to blue and watch the whole page update.

1.3. What’s Already Set Up on Your Lab VM

Your lab machine runs Ubuntu Linux and comes pre-installed with everything you need for this lab. You do not need to install anything.

  • Visual Studio Code — the code editor you used in Weeks 1 and 2.

  • Live Server extension — already installed.

  • Git — this week’s lab folder will get its own fresh Git setup.

  • Firefox and Chrome — either browser can be used. This lab asks you to use Chrome for the DevTools box-model diagram, because its panel is the clearest.

1.4. Open the Folder from the Command Line

You’ll navigate to the lab folder using the terminal, then open it in VS Code from the command line.

  1. Open a terminal. Three ways — use whichever feels easiest:

    • From the dock: Click the Terminal icon in the dock at the bottom of the screen (the black icon with >_).

    • From Activities: Click Activities in the top-left corner, type Terminal in the search box, then click the Terminal icon in the results.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+Alt+T.

  2. Navigate into the lab folder:

    cd ~/Labfiles/evergreen-quote-brand-refresh

     Note

    If you get "No such file or directory," run ls ~/Labfiles to see what’s actually there.

  3. Confirm you’re in the right place:

    ls

    You should see index.html, styles.css, app.js, and README.md.

  4. Open the folder in VS Code from the terminal:

    code .

     Note

    If VS Code asks "Do you trust the authors of the files in this folder?", click Yes, I trust the authors. The Evergreen files are provided by your course.

1.5. Run the App in Chrome

  1. Open the Explorer panel if it is not already visible. Two ways:

    • With the mouse: Click the top icon in the Activity Bar (two overlapping pages).

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+Shift+E.

  2. Right-click on index.html in the Explorer and choose Open with Live Server.

  3. The Evergreen quote page should open in your browser. If it opens in Firefox, right-click the Chrome icon in the dock and open the same URL in Chrome — this lab uses Chrome’s DevTools box-model diagram.

  4. The page should look the same as it did in Week 3: dark-green header, "Coverage that grows with you" hero, and a quote form including the email field you added last week.

  5. Open Chrome DevTools. Two ways:

    • With the mouse: Click the three-dot menu in the top-right of Chrome, then More Tools > Developer Tools.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press F12.

  6. Make sure the Elements tab is selected at the top of DevTools. You will use its Styles panel (on the right) throughout the lab. Click the Styles tab if it’s not already active.

  7. Leave the browser with DevTools open. Switch back to VS Code.

1.6. Initialize Git for This Folder

Just like in previous weeks, you need to tell Git to start tracking this folder before you can see changes.

  1. Open the Source Control panel. Two ways:

    • With the mouse: Click the third icon down in the Activity Bar (a branching line with dots).

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+Shift+G.

  2. Click the Initialize Repository button.

  3. Make an initial commit so there is a baseline to compare future changes against:

    • Click inside the Message box at the top of the Source Control panel.

    • Type Week 4 starting state.

    • Click the blue Commit button. If VS Code asks "Would you like to stage all your changes and commit them directly?", click Yes.

  4. The Source Control panel should now be empty under Changes.

Why this step: A fresh baseline commit lets you view the entire Week 4 change as a single diff at the end of the lab.

1.7. Audit the Hardcoded Brand Color

Before you refactor anything, see the problem with your own eyes.

  1. In the Explorer panel, click styles.css to open it in the editor.

  2. Open the Find box. Two ways:

    • From the menu: Click Edit > Find.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+F.

  3. In the Find box, type #0f5132 (the Evergreen brand green) and press Enter.

  4. Use the up/down arrow buttons next to the Find box to walk through each match. Count them as you go — VS Code will show 6 matches. One is inside a comment at the top of the file; the other five are real style uses:

    • .site-header background (line ~23)

    • .hero h1 text color (line ~48)

    • .quote-form button background (line ~93)

    • .premium-box border-left color (line ~111)

    • .premium-value text color (line ~124)

  5. Now search for #0b3d26 — the darker brand green used for the button’s hover state. You should find one match, inside .quote-form button:hover. This is a separate shade, so it needs its own variable.

    Why this matters: Seven copies of the same color value is small for one file; in a real multi-file stylesheet the count would be in the hundreds. If the brand team says "we’re moving from green to blue," someone has to find and update each one. You are about to make that future change a one-line edit.

  6. Close the Find panel with the X button or by pressing Esc.

1.8. Define CSS Variables at :root

CSS variables — officially called custom properties — are declared inside a rule and used anywhere a value would go. The convention is to declare them on the :root selector at the top of the stylesheet so they apply to the whole page.

  1. At the very top of styles.css (just under the header comment), add a :root block:

    :root {
  --primary: #0f5132;
  --primary-dark: #0b3d26;
  --primary-tint: #e6f0ea;
  --premium-bg: #eef7f1;
  --text: #1f2a2e;
  --text-muted: #3d5a4a;
  --text-faint: #6a7a72;
  --surface: #ffffff;
  --page-bg: #f7f9f8;
  --input-border: #bfcec4;
  --form-padding: 24px;
}

  2. Save the file. Two ways:

    • From the menu: Click File > Save.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+S.

  3. Switch to the browser. The page should look exactly the same as before. You have not changed any visible value — you have only defined variables.

    Why this matters: You introduced indirection without changing behavior. This is the safest possible refactor: add the new mechanism first, prove it doesn’t break anything, then migrate the call sites over to use it. Every experienced engineer works this way.

  4. Switch back to VS Code. You will use the variables you just defined in the next step.

1.9. Replace Hardcoded Colors with var(--…​)

Now you connect each hardcoded color to its new variable home.

  1. In styles.css, find the .site-header rule. Change the background line:

    .site-header {
  ...
  background: var(--primary);   /* was #0f5132 */
  ...
}

  2. Save. Switch to the browser. The header is still green — the variable resolved to #0f5132, and nothing visible changed.

  3. Repeat for every other occurrence. In each rule, replace the hardcoded color with the matching variable:

    • .hero h1 — color: var(--primary);

    • .quote-form button — background: var(--primary);

    • .quote-form button:hover — background: var(--primary-dark);

    • .premium-box — border-left: 4px solid var(--primary);

    • .premium-value — color: var(--primary);

  4. While you’re in the file, migrate the other shared values too:

    • .hero — background: var(--primary-tint);

    • .premium-box — background: var(--premium-bg);

    • body — color: var(--text); and background: var(--page-bg);

    • .tagline, .premium-label — color: var(--text-muted);

    • .site-footer — color: var(--text-faint);

    • .quote-form-section — background: var(--surface); and padding: var(--form-padding);

    • .quote-form input[…​] — border: 1px solid var(--input-border);

  5. Save. Switch to the browser. The page should still look exactly like before. If anything is suddenly the wrong color, use Ctrl+F in styles.css to look for leftover hex codes — you may have missed one.

Why this matters: The page hasn’t visibly changed, but its source is now in a much better shape. Every color lives in exactly one place. Every engineer downstream who edits this file will thank you — and in a few minutes, so will you.

1.10. Rebrand the Entire Page with One Variable Change

Now for the payoff.

  1. At the top of styles.css, change the values of --primary and --primary-dark:

    :root {
  --primary: #0066b2;         /* new corporate blue */
  --primary-dark: #00497f;    /* darker companion */
  ...
}

  2. Save.

  3. Switch to the browser. The entire page should rebrand:

    • The header turns blue.

    • The hero heading "Coverage that grows with you" turns blue.

    • The Calculate Premium button is now blue, with a darker blue hover.

    • The premium box’s left border and value text are both now blue.

      Why this matters: You just rebranded the site in one edit. Production design-system repos work exactly this way — a small tokens.css file defines the brand, and every component stylesheet uses variables declared there. When the brand team ships a refresh, the design-system engineer edits the tokens file, runs the test suite, and opens a PR. That PR, in principle, can change a thousand UI elements with a handful of lines.

  4. Tweak the tint and premium box backgrounds to blue-friendly shades too:

      --primary-tint: #e6f0f7;
  --premium-bg: #eef4f9;

  5. Save and watch the hero band and premium box backgrounds shift from green-ish to blue-ish. The page now feels consistently on-brand.

1.11. Inspect the Box Model in DevTools

You have one more CSS skill to exercise: reading the box model.

  1. Switch to Chrome with DevTools open.

  2. In the Elements panel, click on the .quote-form-section — the white card that holds the form. You can find it two ways:

    • With the mouse: Click the Select Element icon (the arrow in a box, top-left of DevTools), then click the white form card on the page.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+Shift+C, then click the form card.

  3. In the right-hand Styles panel, scroll to the bottom until you see the colored box model diagram: four nested rectangles labeled margin (orange), border (yellow), padding (green), and the content box in the center.

  4. Hover each rectangle. The browser highlights that region on the page in real time.

  5. Double-click the padding value (currently 24) in the DevTools box model diagram. Type 48 and press Enter. Watch the form card visibly grow — more whitespace around every input.

  6. This change is only in DevTools — it will disappear when you reload the page. That’s fine: DevTools is for experimenting. You just wanted to see what a padding change would look like before making it permanent.

1.12. Make the Padding Change Permanent

Now put that padding change into your actual stylesheet.

  1. Switch back to VS Code with styles.css open.

  2. At the top of the file, change --form-padding from 24px to 32px:

      --form-padding: 32px;   /* was 24px — more breathing room */

  3. Save. Switch to the browser. The form card should now have noticeably more space around its inputs — not as dramatic as the 48px DevTools experiment, but visibly roomier.

Why this matters: You made a real spacing change through a variable. If your team ever decides "every form on the site should have more breathing room," updating --form-padding in one place applies the change site-wide.

1.13. See the Whole Change in Git

  1. Open the Source Control panel. Two ways:

    • With the mouse: Click the third icon down in the Activity Bar.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+Shift+G.

  2. styles.css should appear under Changes with an M.

  3. Click styles.css to open the diff view. Before and after side by side — you’ll see the new :root block on the right, and the hardcoded colors replaced with var(--…​) calls throughout.

  4. Close the diff tab.

  5. Open VS Code’s integrated terminal. Two ways:

    • From the menu: Click Terminal > New Terminal.

    • With a keyboard shortcut: Press Ctrl+` .

  6. Run git diff --stat:

    git diff --stat

    You should see one file changed — styles.css — with a modest line count of insertions and deletions.

Why this matters: Even though the visual change is dramatic (whole-site rebrand), the diff is small and focused. That’s the hallmark of a good refactor: big outcome, small surface area.

1.14. Commit the Change

  1. In the Source Control panel, click inside the Message box and type:

    Refactor styles to use CSS variables; rebrand to corporate blue

  2. Click the blue Commit button. If VS Code asks to stage all your changes, click Yes.

  3. Run git status in the terminal — it should say nothing to commit, working tree clean.

  4. Run git log --oneline:

    git log --oneline

    You should see your two commits — Week 4 starting state and Refactor styles to use CSS variables; rebrand to corporate blue.

Why this matters: A commit message like "Refactor styles to use CSS variables; rebrand to corporate blue" tells a future teammate exactly what this commit did and why. "Updated colors" would have told them nothing. Good commit messages add up to a changelog your team can actually use.

1.15. Review

In this lab, you:

  • Opened the Evergreen stylesheet and counted hardcoded brand-color duplication.

  • Added a :root block with CSS variables for the brand palette and common values.

  • Replaced every hardcoded color in the stylesheet with a var(--…​) reference, without changing anything visible.

  • Changed two variable values and rebranded the entire site from Evergreen green to a new corporate blue.

  • Used Chrome DevTools to read and experiment with the box model of the form card.

  • Bumped --form-padding to tighten up the form visually.

  • Reviewed the full change as a Git diff and committed it with a clear message.

You wrote no new JavaScript, added no new HTML elements, and changed roughly fifteen lines of CSS — but you rebranded a website. That is the leverage CSS variables give a design system.

Next week you’ll revisit this same page in the Flexbox / Responsive Design module to make the header layout adapt to small screens.
